读取本仓库 macOS 构建/设计相关的本地基线资料,不联网:
1) Tools/agent-platform-plugin-refs/ 下 build-macos-apps 相关的 SKILL.md 与 references(先 ls 看结构再挑重点读);
2) docs/ 下与 UIUE 相关的既有决策(可 grep "UIUE" docs/ 快速定位,重点看 roadmap-2026-07-07-macos-closure-baseline.md 中 UIUE 相关段落)。
输出中文:本地已有哪些 macOS UI 开发方法论资产、已锁的 UIUE 相关决策/约束、外部调研应该补什么缺口。findings 的 source_url 用本地文件路径(file:line)。

attachment.content:
- archive-research-pack: Use when 一次多路 workflow 调研 / deep-research / pre-mortem oracle 搜证收口,需要把成果落盘防失忆。症状:调研完只想存一个总报告、transcript 在 /tmp 待清会丢、finder 引的 source URL 事后无法复核、各路一手档与综合报告混在一起。
- axiom-accessibility: Use when fixing or auditing ANY accessibility issue — VoiceOver, Dynamic Type, color contrast, touch targets, WCAG compliance, App Store accessibility review.
- axiom-ai: Use when implementing ANY Apple Intelligence or on-device AI feature. Covers Foundation Models, @Generable, LanguageModelSession, structured output, Tool protocol, iOS 26 AI integration.
- axiom-apple-docs: Use when ANY question involves Apple framework APIs, Swift compiler errors, or Xcode-bundled documentation. Covers Liquid Glass, Swift 6.2 concurrency, Foundation Models, SwiftData, StoreKit, 32 Swift compiler diagnostics.
- axiom-build: Use when ANY iOS build fails, test crashes, Xcode misbehaves, or environment issue occurs before debugging code. Covers build failures, compilation errors, dependency conflicts, simulator problems, environment-first diagnostics.
- axiom-concurrency: Use when writing ANY async code, actors, threads, or seeing ANY concurrency error. Covers Swift 6 concurrency, @MainActor, Sendable, data races, async/await patterns.
- axiom-data: Use when working with ANY data persistence, database, storage, CloudKit, migration, or serialization. Covers SwiftData, Core Data, GRDB, SQLite, CloudKit sync, file storage, Codable, migrations.
- axiom-design: Use when making design decisions, implementing HIG patterns, Liquid Glass, SF Symbols, typography, or structuring app entry points and authentication flows.
- axiom-games: Use when building ANY 2D or 3D game with SpriteKit, SceneKit, or RealityKit, or adding touch controls or game controller support. Covers scene graphs, ECS, physics, actions, game loops, rendering, TouchController, GCController.
- axiom-graphics: Use when working with ANY GPU rendering, Metal, OpenGL migration, shaders, 3D content, RealityKit, AR, USD/USDZ files, or display performance. Covers Metal migration, shader conversion, RealityKit ECS, RealityView, USDKit, ProMotion.
- axiom-health: Use when working with HealthKit, WorkoutKit, health data, workouts, or fitness features on iOS or watchOS. Covers permissions, queries, background delivery, custom workouts, multidevice coordination.
- axiom-integration: Use when integrating ANY iOS system feature - Siri, Shortcuts, widgets, IAP, localization, privacy, alarms, calendar, reminders, contacts, background tasks, push notifications, timers. Covers App Intents, WidgetKit, StoreKit, EventKit, Contacts.
- axiom-location: Use when implementing location services, maps, geofencing, or debugging location/MapKit issues. Covers Core Location, CLMonitor, MapKit, annotations, directions.
- axiom-macos: Use when building ANY macOS app — windows, menus, sandboxing, distribution, AppKit bridging or modernization (control events, state restoration, concentric corners), or macOS-specific SwiftUI patterns.
- axiom-media: Use when working with camera, photos, audio, haptics, ShazamKit, or Now Playing. Covers AVCaptureSession, PHPicker, PhotosPicker, AVFoundation, Core Haptics, audio recognition, MediaPlayer, CarPlay, MusicKit.
- axiom-networking: Use when implementing or debugging ANY network connection, API call, or socket. Covers URLSession, Network.framework, NetworkConnection, connection diagnostics.
- axiom-payments: Use when accepting ANY real-world payment — Apple Pay, Wallet passes, Tap to Pay, Orders in Wallet. NOT in-app purchase / digital content (use axiom-integration). Covers entitlements, certs, signing, App Review payment rules.
- axiom-performance: Use when app feels slow, memory grows, battery drains, or diagnosing ANY performance issue. Covers memory leaks, profiling, Instruments workflows, retain cycles, performance optimization.
- axiom-security: Use when storing credentials securely, encrypting data, implementing passkeys, securing AI/agentic features against prompt injection, code signing, or managing certificates and provisioning profiles.
- axiom-shipping: Use when preparing ANY app for submission, handling App Store rejections, writing appeals, or managing App Store Connect. Covers submission checklists, rejection troubleshooting, metadata requirements, privacy manifests, age ratings, export compliance.
- axiom-swift: Use when reviewing Swift code for modern idioms, working with noncopyable types, implementing drag and drop, adding debug deep links, or building for tvOS.
- axiom-swiftui: Use when building, fixing, or improving ANY SwiftUI UI — views, navigation, layout, animations, performance, architecture, gestures, debugging, iOS 26 features.
- axiom-testing: Use when writing ANY test, debugging flaky tests, making tests faster, or choosing Swift Testing vs XCTest. Covers unit tests, UI tests, async testing, test architecture.
- axiom-tools: Use when asking how to use Axiom or what skills exist, capturing console with xclog, symbolicating .ips/MetricKit/.crash crashes with xcsym, driving/validating simulator UI & accessibility with xcui, or analyzing xctrace/CPU profiles with xcprof.
- axiom-uikit: Use when bridging UIKit and SwiftUI, modernizing UIKit apps (scene lifecycle, resizability), debugging Auto Layout, Combine, TextKit, PencilKit, or UIKit animations.
- axiom-vision: Use when implementing ANY computer vision feature — image analysis, pose detection, person segmentation, subject lifting, text recognition, barcode scanning.
- axiom-watchos: Use when building ANY watchOS app — app structure, independent apps, Watch Connectivity, Smart Stack widgets, complications, controls, RelevanceKit, background tasks, ClockKit migration.
- axiom-xcode-mcp: Use when connecting to Xcode via MCP, using xcrun mcpbridge, or working with ANY Xcode MCP tool (XcodeRead, BuildProject, RunTests, RenderPreview). Covers setup, tool reference, workflow patterns, troubleshooting.
- closeout-receipt-writer: Use when 一个 apply/重型任务/审计 wave 收口需要写 receipt 证据。症状:想用 hardcoded pass、读 metadata flag 自证 verdict、凭印象写状态、或 receipt 与实跑 log 脱节;0-34 灾难根因正是 receipt 读自己的 metadata flag 翻 pass、零 token 证据。
- doc-cascade-sweep: Use when 范式翻案/架构大改/口径回写后,grep 全仓命中几十个文件含过期锚点或旧数字,需要全仓级联更新。症状:看到 grep 命中 60+ 文件就想 sed 全量替换、或一个决策只 append 进一个文件没回写文档组、或改了带日期的历史快照正文破坏一手溯源。
- ios-debugger-agent: Build, run, and debug iOS apps on Simulator with XcodeBuildMCP. Use when launching an app, inspecting simulator UI or logs, or diagnosing runtime behavior.
- ios-ettrace-performance: Capture and interpret iOS Simulator ETTrace profiles. Use when profiling launch or runtime latency, comparing traces, or finding CPU-heavy stacks.
- ios-simulator-skill: 29 production-ready scripts for iOS app testing, building, and automation. Provides semantic UI navigation, build automation, accessibility testing, and simulator lifecycle management. Optimized for AI agents with minimal token output.
- openspec-apply-change: Implement tasks from an OpenSpec change. Use when the user wants to start implementing, continue implementation, or work through tasks.
- openspec-archive-change: Archive a completed change in the experimental workflow. Use when the user wants to finalize and archive a change after implementation is complete.
- openspec-explore: Enter explore mode - a thinking partner for exploring ideas, investigating problems, and clarifying requirements. Use when the user wants to think through something before or during a change.
- openspec-propose: Propose a new change with all artifacts generated in one step. Use when the user wants to quickly describe what they want to build and get a complete proposal with design, specs, and tasks ready for implementation.
- openspec-sync-specs: Sync delta specs from a change to main specs. Use when the user wants to update main specs with changes from a delta spec, without archiving the change.
- verify-external-claims: Use when 一个多路 workflow/finder 调研、deep-research、或综合官报告引用了 arxiv ID、精确数字（调用数/百分比/star/benchmark）、或 repo 活跃度断言,且这些要驱动决策。症状:finder 在搜证压力下高发编造精确数字 + 引用 ID,且常安到真实存在的 source 上(issue/论文真、数字假),综合官即使做了本机 grep 也漏外部声称。
- opsx:apply: Implement tasks from an OpenSpec change (Experimental)
- opsx:archive: Archive a completed change in the experimental workflow
- opsx:explore: Enter explore mode - think through ideas, investigate problems, clarify requirements
- opsx:propose: Propose a new change - create it and generate all artifacts in one step
- opsx:sync: Sync delta specs from a change to main specs
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

# Agent Platform Plugin References

Local symlink index for agents working on iOS/macOS frontend, runtime, visual, simulator, or packaging tasks.

Read this directory before SwiftUI, iOS, macOS, Liquid Glass, simulator, performance, signing, or app packaging work.

## Worktree iOS Build Default

This main worktree is preconfigured for Codex `build-ios-apps`.

- Default profile: `ios`
- Project: `MAformac.xcodeproj`
- Scheme: `MAformacIOS`
- Dedicated simulator: `iPhone 17 Pro`
- Local config file: `.xcodebuildmcp/config.yaml`

New Codex windows in this worktree should use this order:

1. Read this file and `.xcodebuildmcp/README.md`.
2. Call `session_show_defaults`.
3. If the active profile is not `ios`, call `session_use_defaults_profile({ profile: "ios" })`.
4. Build/run with `build_run_sim()`.

Do not point this worktree at the UIUE simulator by default. Both worktrees currently use the same iOS bundle id, so sharing one simulator causes install overwrite churn.

## Symlink Targets

- `build-ios-apps-plugin` -> `/Users/wanglei/.codex/plugins/cache/openai-curated-remote/build-ios-apps/0.1.2`
- `build-ios-apps-skills` -> `/Users/wanglei/.codex/plugins/cache/openai-curated-remote/build-ios-apps/0.1.2/skills`

# SwiftUI Patterns

## Quick Start

Choose a track based on your goal:

### Existing project

- Identify the feature or scene and the primary interaction model: document, editor, sidebar-detail, utility window, settings, or menu bar extra.
- Read the nearest existing scene or root view before inventing a new desktop structure.
- Choose the relevant reference from `references/components-index.md`.
- If SwiftUI cannot express the required platform behavior cleanly, use the `appkit-interop` skill rather than forcing a shaky workaround.

### New app scaffolding

=== plugin README ===
# Build macOS Apps Plugin

This plugin packages macOS-first development workflows in `plugins/build-macos-apps`.

It currently includes these skills:

- `build-run-debug`
- `test-triage`
- `signing-entitlements`
- `swiftpm-macos`
- `packaging-notarization`
- `swiftui-patterns`
- `liquid-glass`
- `window-management`
- `appkit-interop`
- `view-refactor`
- `telemetry`

## What It Covers

- discovering local Xcode workspaces, projects, and Swift packages
- building and running macOS apps with shell-first desktop workflows
- creating one project-local `script/build_and_run.sh` entrypoint and wiring `.codex/environments/environment.toml` so the Codex app Run button works
- implementing native macOS SwiftUI scenes, menus, settings, toolbars, and multiwindow flows
- adopting modern macOS Liquid Glass and design-system guidance with standard SwiftUI structures, toolbars, search, controls, and custom glass surfaces
- tailoring SwiftUI windows with title/toolbar styling, material-backed container backgrounds, minimize/restoration behavior, default and ideal placement, borderless window style, and launch behavior
- bridging into AppKit for representables, responder-chain behavior, panels, and other desktop-only needs
- refactoring large macOS view files toward stable scene, selection, and command structure
- adding lightweight `Logger` / `os.Logger` instrumentation for windows, sidebars, menu commands, and menu bar actions
- reading and verifying runtime events with Console, `log stream`, and process logs
- triaging failing unit, integration, and UI-hosted macOS tests
- debugging launch failures, crashes, linker problems, and runtime regressions
- inspecting signing identities, entitlements, hardened runtime, and Gatekeeper issues
- preparing packaging and notarization workflows for distribution

## What It Does Not Cover

- iOS, watchOS, or tvOS simulator control
- desktop UI automation
- App Store Connect release management
- pixel-perfect visual design or design-system generation

## Plugin Structure

The plugin lives at:

- `plugins/build-macos-apps/`

with this shape:

- `.codex-plugin/plugin.json`
  - required plugin manifest
  - defines plugin metadata and points Codex at the plugin contents

- `agents/`
  - plugin-level agent metadata
  - currently includes `agents/openai.yaml` for the OpenAI surface

- `commands/`
  - reusable workflow entrypoints for common macOS development tasks

- `skills/`
  - the actual skill payload
  - each skill keeps the normal skill structure (`SKILL.md`, optional
    `agents/`, `references/`, `assets/`, `scripts/`)

## Notes

This plugin is currently skills-first at the plugin level. It does not ship a
plugin-local `.mcp.json`, matching the public `plugins/build-ios-apps` shape.

The default posture is shell-first. Unlike the iOS build plugin, this plugin
does not assume simulator tooling or touch-driven UI inspection for its main
workflows. The core execution model leans on `xcodebuild`, `swift`, `open`,
`lldb`, `codesign`, `spctl`, `plutil`, and `log stream`, with a compact desktop
UI layer for native SwiftUI scene design, AppKit interop, and macOS-specific
refactoring.

1	########## swiftui-patterns/SKILL.md ##########
2	---
3	name: swiftui-patterns
4	description: Build macOS SwiftUI scenes and components with desktop patterns. Use when shaping windows, commands, toolbars, settings, split views, or inspectors.
5	---
6	
7	# SwiftUI Patterns
8	
9	## Quick Start
10	
11	Choose a track based on your goal:
12	
13	### Existing project
14	
15	- Identify the feature or scene and the primary interaction model: document, editor, sidebar-detail, utility window, settings, or menu bar extra.
16	- Read the nearest existing scene or root view before inventing a new desktop structure.
17	- Choose the relevant reference from `references/components-index.md`.
18	- If SwiftUI cannot express the required platform behavior cleanly, use the `appkit-interop` skill rather than forcing a shaky workaround.
19	
20	### New app scaffolding
21	
22	- Choose the scene model first: `WindowGroup`, `Window`, `Settings`, `MenuBarExtra`, or `DocumentGroup`.
23	- If the app combines a normal main window and a `MenuBarExtra`, use `WindowGroup(..., id:)` for the primary window when it should appear at launch. Treat `Window(...)` as a better fit for auxiliary/on-demand singleton windows; in menu-bar-heavy apps, a `Window(...)` scene may not present the main window automatically at launch.
24	- Before creating the scaffold, check whether the workspace is already inside a git repo with `git rev-parse --is-inside-work-tree`. If not, run `git init` at the project root so Codex app git-backed features are available from the start. Do not initialize a nested repo inside an existing parent checkout.
25	- For a new app scaffold, also create one project-local `script/build_and_run.sh` and `.codex/environments/environment.toml` so the Codex app Run button works immediately. Use the exact bootstrap contract from `build-run-debug` and its `references/run-button-bootstrap.md` file rather than inventing a second variant here.
26	- Decide which state is app-wide, scene-scoped, or window-scoped before writing views.
27	- Sketch file and module boundaries before writing the full UI. For any non-trivial app, create the folder structure first and split files by responsibility from the start.
28	- Use a single Swift file only for tiny throwaway examples or snippets: roughly under 50 lines, one screen, no persistence, no networking/process client, and no reusable models. Anything beyond that should be multi-file immediately.
29	- Use system-adaptive colors and materials by default (`Color.primary`, `Color.secondary`, semantic foreground styles, `.regularMaterial`, etc.) so the app follows Light/Dark mode automatically. Do not hardcode white or light backgrounds unless the user explicitly asks for a fixed theme, and do not reach for opaque `windowBackgroundColor` fills for root panes by default.
30	- Pick the references for the first feature surface you need: windowing, commands, split layouts, or settings.
31	
32	## New App File Structure
33	
34	For any non-trivial macOS app, start with this shape instead of putting the app,
35	all views, models, stores, services, and helpers in one Swift file:
36	
37	- `App/<AppName>App.swift`: the `@main` app type and `AppDelegate` only.
38	- `Views/ContentView.swift`: root layout and high-level composition only.
39	- `Views/SidebarView.swift`, `Views/DetailView.swift`, `Views/ComposerView.swift`, etc.: feature views named after their primary type.
40	- `Models/*.swift`: value models, identifiers, and selection enums.
41	- `Stores/*.swift`: persistence and state stores.
42	- `Services/*.swift`: app-server, network, process, or platform clients.
43	- `Support/*.swift`: small formatters, resolvers, extensions, and glue helpers.
44	
45	Keep files small and named after the primary type they contain. If a file starts
46	collecting unrelated views, models, stores, networking clients, and helper
47	extensions, split it before adding more behavior.
48	
49	## Pre-Edit Checklist For New App Scaffolds
50	
51	Before writing the full UI:
52	
53	1. Choose the scene model.
54	2. Choose state ownership: app-wide, scene-scoped, window-scoped, or view-local.
55	3. Sketch file and module boundaries.
56	4. Create the folder structure before filling in the UI.
57	5. Keep `script/build_and_run.sh` and `.codex/environments/environment.toml` separate from app source.
58	
59	## General Rules To Follow
60	
61	- Design for pointer, keyboard, menus, and multiple windows.
62	- Keep scenes explicit. A separate settings window, utility window, or menu bar extra should be modeled as its own scene, not hidden inside one monolithic `ContentView`.
63	- Prefer system desktop affordances: `commands`, toolbars, sidebars, inspectors, contextual menus, and `searchable`.
64	- For menu bar apps, keep `MenuBarExtra` item titles and action labels short and scannable. Cap visible menu item text at 30 characters; if source content is longer, truncate or summarize it before rendering and open the full content in a dedicated window or detail surface.
65	- If a `MenuBarExtra` app should still behave like a regular Dock app with a visible main window/process, install an `NSApplicationDelegate` via `@NSApplicationDelegateAdaptor`, call `NSApp.setActivationPolicy(.regular)` during launch, and activate the app with `NSApp.activate(ignoringOtherApps: true)`. If the app is intentionally menu-bar-only, document that `.accessory` / no-Dock behavior is a deliberate product choice.
66	- Prefer system-adaptive colors, materials, and semantic foreground styles. Avoid fixed white/light backgrounds in scaffolding and examples unless the requested design explicitly calls for a custom non-adaptive theme.
67	- Do not paint `NavigationSplitView` sidebars or root window panes with opaque custom `Color(...)` or `Color(nsColor: .windowBackgroundColor)` fills by default. Prefer native macOS sidebar/window materials and system-provided backgrounds unless the user explicitly asks for a custom opaque surface. In sidebar-detail-inspector layouts, let the sidebar keep the standard source-list/material appearance and reserve custom backgrounds for detail or inspector content cards where needed.
68	- Use `@SceneStorage` for per-window ephemeral state and `@AppStorage` for durable user preferences.
69	- Keep selection state explicit and stable. macOS layouts often pivot around sidebar selection rather than push navigation.
70	- Prefer `NavigationSplitView` or a deliberate manual split layout over iOS-style stacked flows when the app benefits from always-visible structure.
71	- For `List(...).listStyle(.sidebar)` and `NavigationSplitView` sidebars, prefer flat native rows with standard system selection/highlight behavior. Keep rows visually lightweight and Mail-like: at most one leading icon, one strong title line, and one optional secondary detail line in `.secondary`. Avoid stacked metadata rows, repeated inline utility icons, or dense multi-column status text in the sidebar. Reserve card-style and metadata-heavy surfaces for detail or inspector panes unless the user explicitly asks for a highly custom sidebar treatment.
72	- Keep primary actions discoverable from both UI chrome and keyboard shortcuts when appropriate.
73	- Use SwiftUI-native scenes and views first. If you need low-level window, responder-chain, text system, or panel control, switch to `appkit-interop`.
74	
75	For concrete sidebar row and split-view background examples, read
76	`references/split-inspectors.md`.
77	
78	## State Ownership Summary
79	
80	Use the narrowest state tool that matches the ownership model:
81	
82	| Scenario | Preferred pattern |
83	| --- | --- |
84	| Local view or control state | `@State` |
85	| Child mutates parent-owned value state | `@Binding` |
86	| Root-owned reference model on macOS 14+ | `@State` with an `@Observable` type |
87	| Child reads or mutates an injected `@Observable` model | Pass it explicitly as a stored property |
88	| Window-scoped ephemeral selection or expansion state | `@SceneStorage` when practical, otherwise scene-owned `@State` |
89	| Shared user preference | `@AppStorage` |
90	| Shared app service or configuration | `@Environment(Type.self)` |
91	| Legacy reference model on older targets | `@StateObject` at the owner and `@ObservedObject` when injected |
92	
93	Choose the ownership location first, then the wrapper. Do not turn simple desktop state into a view model by reflex.
94	
95	## Cross-Cutting References
96	
97	- `references/components-index.md`: entry point for scene and component guidance.
98	- `references/windowing.md`: choosing between `WindowGroup`, `Window`, `DocumentGroup`, and window-opening patterns.
99	- `references/settings.md`: dedicated settings scenes, `SettingsLink`, and preference layouts.
100	- `references/commands-menus.md`: command menus, keyboard shortcuts, focused values, and desktop action routing.
101	- `references/split-inspectors.md`: sidebars, split views, selection-driven layout, and inspectors.
102	- `references/menu-bar-extra.md`: menu bar extra structure and when it fits.
103	
104	## Anti-Patterns
105	
106	- One huge `ContentView` pretending the whole app is a single screen.
107	- A single Swift file containing the `@main` app, all views, models, stores, networking/process clients, formatters, and extensions. This is acceptable only for tiny throwaway snippets under the new-app threshold above.
108	- Touch-first interaction models ported directly from iOS without desktop affordances.
109	- Hiding core actions behind gestures with no menu, toolbar, or keyboard path.
110	- Building a menu-bar-plus-window app around only a `Window(...)` scene and then expecting the main window to appear at launch. Use `WindowGroup(..., id:)` for the primary launch window and reserve `Window(...)` for auxiliary/on-demand windows.
111	- Rendering full unbounded document titles, prompts, or message text directly inside a menu bar extra. Menu item labels should stay at or below 30 characters, with longer content moved into a dedicated window or detail view.
112	- Treating settings as another navigation destination in the main content window.
113	- Hardcoding `.background(.white)`, `Color.white`, or a fixed light palette in a brand-new scaffold without an explicit design requirement.
114	- Wrapping each sidebar item in large rounded custom cards inside a `.sidebar` list, which fights native source-list density, alignment, and selection behavior unless the user explicitly asked for a bespoke visual sidebar.
115	- Building sidebar rows with multiple repeated icons, three or more text lines, or a dense strip of inline metadata counters/timestamps/models. Keep the sidebar row to one icon and one or two text lines, then move richer metadata into the detail pane.
116	- Painting `NavigationSplitView` sidebars or root window panes with opaque custom color fills by default, instead of letting the sidebar use native source-list/material appearance and reserving custom backgrounds for actual content cards.
117	- Using push navigation for layouts that want stable sidebar selection and detail panes.
118	- Reaching for AppKit before the SwiftUI scene and command APIs have been used properly.
119	
120	## Workflow For A New macOS Scene Or View
121	
122	1. Define the scene type and ownership model before writing child views.
123	2. Decide which actions live in content, toolbars, commands, inspectors, or settings.
124	3. Sketch the selection model and layout: sidebar-detail, editor-inspector, document window, or utility window.
125	4. Create the file/folder structure for app entrypoint, root layout, feature views, models, stores, services, and support helpers.
126	5. Build with small, focused subviews and explicit inputs rather than giant computed fragments.
127	6. Add keyboard shortcuts and menu or toolbar exposure for actions that matter on desktop.
128	7. Validate the flow with a build and a quick usability pass: multiwindow assumptions, settings entry points, and selection stability.
129	
130	## Component References
131	
132	Use `references/components-index.md` as the entry point. Each component reference should include:
133	- intent and best-fit scenarios
134	- minimal usage pattern with desktop conventions
135	- pitfalls and discoverability notes
136	- when to fall back to `appkit-interop`
137	
138	########## liquid-glass/SKILL.md ##########
139	---
140	name: liquid-glass
141	description: Implement and review macOS SwiftUI Liquid Glass UI. Use when adopting system glass, removing conflicting custom chrome, or building glass surfaces.
142	---
143	
144	# Liquid Glass
145	
146	## Overview
147	
148	Use this skill to bring a macOS SwiftUI app into the modern macOS design system
149	with the least custom chrome possible. Start with standard app structure,
150	toolbars, search placement, sheets, and controls, then add custom Liquid Glass
151	only where the app needs a distinctive surface.
152	
153	Prefer system-provided glass and adaptive materials over bespoke blur, opaque
154	backgrounds, or custom toolbar/sidebar skins. Audit existing UI for extra fills,
155	scrims, and clipping before adding more effects.
156	
157	## Workflow
158	
159	1. Read the relevant scene or root view and identify the structural pattern:
160	   `NavigationSplitView`, `TabView`, sheet presentation, detail/inspector
161	   layout, toolbar, or custom floating controls.
162	2. Remove custom backgrounds or darkening layers behind system sheets,
163	   sidebars, and toolbars unless the product explicitly needs them. These can
164	   obscure Liquid Glass and interfere with the automatic scroll-edge effect.
165	3. Update standard SwiftUI structure and controls first.
166	4. Add custom `glassEffect` surfaces only for app-specific UI that standard
167	   controls do not cover.
168	5. Validate that glass grouping, transitions, icon treatment, and foreground
169	   activation are visually coherent and still usable with pointer and keyboard.
170	6. If the UI change also affects launch behavior for a SwiftPM GUI app, use
171	   `build-run-debug` so the app runs as a foreground `.app` bundle rather
172	   than as a raw executable.
173	
174	## App Structure
175	
176	- Prefer `NavigationSplitView` for hierarchy-driven macOS layouts. Let the
177	  sidebar use the system Liquid Glass material instead of painting over it.
178	- For hero artwork or large media adjacent to a floating sidebar, use
179	  `backgroundExtensionEffect` so the visual can extend beyond the safe area
180	  without clipping the subject.
181	- Keep inspectors visually associated with the current selection and avoid
182	  giving them a heavier custom background than the content they inspect.
183	- If the app uses tabs, keep `TabView` for persistent top-level sections and
184	  preserve each tab's local navigation state.
185	- Do not force iPhone-only tab bar minimize/accessory behavior onto a Mac app.
186	  On macOS, prefer a conventional top toolbar and native tab/search placement.
187	- If a sheet already uses `presentationBackground` purely to imitate frosted
188	  material, consider removing it and letting the system's new material render.
189	- For sheet transitions that should visually originate from a toolbar button,
190	  make the presenting item the source of a navigation zoom transition and mark
191	  the sheet content as the destination.
192	
193	## Toolbars
194	
195	- Assume toolbar items are rendered on a floating Liquid Glass surface and are
196	  grouped automatically.
197	- Use `ToolbarSpacer` to communicate grouping:
198	  - fixed spacing to split related actions into a distinct group,
199	  - flexible spacing to push a leading action away from a trailing group.
200	- Use `sharedBackgroundVisibility` when an item should stand alone without the
201	  shared glass background, for example a profile/avatar item.
202	- Add `badge` to toolbar item content for notification or status indicators.
203	- Expect monochrome icon rendering in more toolbar contexts. Use `tint` only to
204	  convey semantic meaning such as a primary action or alert state, not as pure
205	  decoration.
206	- If content underneath a toolbar has extra darkening, blur, or custom
207	  background layers, remove them before judging the new automatic scroll-edge
208	  effect.
209	- For dense windows with many floating elements, tune the content's scroll-edge
210	  treatment with `scrollEdgeEffectStyle` instead of building a custom bar
211	  background.
212	
213	## Search
214	
215	- For a search field that applies across a whole split-view hierarchy, attach
216	  `searchable` to the `NavigationSplitView`, not to just one column.
217	- When search is secondary and a compact affordance is better, use
218	  `searchToolbarBehavior` instead of hand-rolling a toolbar button and a
219	  separate field.
220	- For a dedicated search page in a multi-tab app, assign the search role to one
221	  tab and place `searchable` on the `TabView`.
222	- Make most of the app's content discoverable from search when the field lives
223	  in the top-trailing toolbar location.
224	- On iPad and Mac, expect the dedicated search tab to show a centered field
225	  above browsing suggestions rather than a bottom search bar.
226	
227	## Controls
228	
229	- Prefer standard SwiftUI controls before creating custom glass components.
230	- Expect bordered buttons to default to a capsule shape at larger sizes. On
231	  macOS, mini/small/medium controls preserve a rounded-rectangle shape for
232	  denser layouts.
233	- Use `buttonBorderShape` when a button shape needs to be explicit.
234	- Use `controlSize` to preserve density in inspectors and popovers, and reserve
235	  extra-large sizing for truly prominent actions.
236	- Use the system glass and glass-prominent button styles for primary actions
237	  instead of recreating a translucent button background by hand.
238	- For sliders with discrete values, pass `step` to get automatic tick marks or
239	  provide specific ticks in a `ticks` closure.
240	- For sliders that should expand left and right around a baseline, set
241	  `neutralValue`.
242	- Use `Label` or standard control initializers for menu items so icons are
243	  consistently placed on the leading edge across platforms.
244	- For custom shapes that must align concentrically with a sheet, card, or
245	  window corner, use a concentric rectangle shape with the
246	  `containerConcentric` corner configuration instead of guessing a radius.
247	
248	## Custom Liquid Glass
249	
250	- Use `glassEffect` for custom glass surfaces. The default shape is capsule-like
251	  and text foregrounds are automatically made vibrant and legible against
252	  changing content underneath.
253	- Pass an explicit shape to `glassEffect` when a capsule is not the right fit.
254	- Add `tint` only when color carries meaning, such as a status or call to
255	  action.
256	- Use `glassEffect(... .interactive())` for custom controls or containers with
257	  interactive elements so they scale, bounce, and shimmer like system glass.
258	- Wrap nearby custom glass elements in one `GlassEffectContainer`. This is a
259	  visual correctness rule, not just organization: separate containers cannot
260	  sample each other's glass and can produce inconsistent refraction.
261	- Use `glassEffectID` with a local `@Namespace` when matching glass elements
262	  should morph between collapsed and expanded states.
263	
264	## Review Checklist
265	
266	- Standard structures and controls were updated first before adding custom
267	  glass.
268	- Opaque backgrounds, dark scrims, and custom toolbar/sheet fills that fight the
269	  system material were removed unless intentionally required.
270	- `searchable` is attached at the correct container level for the intended
271	  search scope.
272	- Toolbar grouping uses `ToolbarSpacer`, `sharedBackgroundVisibility`, and
273	  `badge` instead of one-off hand-built chrome.
274	- Icon tint is semantic, not decorative.
275	- Custom glass elements that sit near each other share a
276	  `GlassEffectContainer`.
277	- Morphing glass transitions use `glassEffectID` with a namespace and stable
278	  identity.
279	- Any SwiftPM GUI app used to test the result is launched as a `.app` bundle,
280	  not as a raw executable.
281	
282	## Guardrails
283	
284	- Do not rebuild system sidebars, toolbars, sheets, or controls from scratch if
285	  standard SwiftUI APIs already provide the modern macOS behavior.
286	- Do not apply custom opaque backgrounds behind a `NavigationSplitView`
287	  sidebar, system toolbar, or sheet just because an older version needed
288	  one.
289	- Do not scatter related glass elements across multiple
290	  `GlassEffectContainer`s.
291	- Do not tint every icon or glass surface for visual variety alone.
292	- Do not assume an iPhone tab/search behavior is the right answer on macOS.
293	  Prefer desktop-native toolbar, split-view, and inspector placement.
294	- Do not leave a GUI SwiftPM app launching as a bare executable when reviewing
295	  Liquid Glass behavior; missing foreground activation can make a design bug
296	  look like a rendering bug.
297	
298	## When To Use Other Skills
299	
300	- Use `swiftui-patterns` when the main question is scene architecture,
301	  sidebar/detail layout, commands, or settings rather than Liquid Glass-specific
302	  treatment.
303	- Use `view-refactor` when the main issue is file structure, state
304	  ownership, and extracting large views before design changes.
305	- Use `appkit-interop` when the design requires window, panel, responder-chain,
306	  or AppKit-only control behavior.
307	- Use `build-run-debug` when you need to launch, verify, or inspect logs
308	  for the app after the visual update.
309	
310	########## window-management/SKILL.md ##########
311	---
312	name: window-management
313	description: Customize macOS SwiftUI windows and scene behavior. Use when tuning window chrome, drag regions, placement, restoration, launch behavior, or borderless windows.
314	---
315	
316	# Window Management
317	
318	## Overview
319	
320	Use this skill to tailor each SwiftUI window to its job. Start by identifying
321	which scene owns the window (`Window`, `WindowGroup`, or a dedicated utility
322	scene), then customize the toolbar/title area, background material, resize and
323	restoration behavior, and initial or zoomed placement.
324	
325	Prefer scene and window modifiers over ad hoc AppKit bridges when SwiftUI offers
326	the behavior directly. Keep each window purpose-built: a main browser window, an
327	About window, and a media player window usually want different chrome,
328	resizability, restoration, and placement rules.
329	
330	These APIs are macOS 15+ SwiftUI window/scene customizations. For older
331	deployment targets, expect to use more AppKit bridging or availability guards.
332	
333	## Workflow
334	
335	1. Inspect the relevant scene declaration and classify the window role:
336	   main app navigation, inspector/detail utility, About/support window, media
337	   playback window, welcome window, or a borderless custom surface.
338	2. Adjust toolbar and title presentation to match the content.
339	3. If the toolbar background or entire toolbar is hidden, make sure the window
340	   still has a usable drag region.
341	4. Refine window behavior for that role: minimize availability, restoration,
342	   resize expectations, and whether the window should appear at launch.
343	5. Set default placement for newly opened windows and ideal placement for zoom
344	   behavior when content and display size matter.
345	6. Build and launch the app with `build-run-debug` to verify the result in
346	   a real foreground `.app` bundle.
347	7. If SwiftUI scene/window modifiers are not enough, switch to `appkit-interop`
348	   for a narrow `NSWindow` bridge rather than spreading AppKit through the view
349	   tree.
350	
351	## Toolbar And Title
352	
353	- Use `.toolbar(removing: .title)` when the window title should stay associated
354	  with the window for accessibility and menus, but not be visibly drawn in the
355	  title bar.
356	- Use `.toolbarBackgroundVisibility(.hidden, for: .windowToolbar)` when large
357	  media or hero content should visually extend to the top edge of the window.
358	- If the window still needs close/minimize/full-screen controls, remove only the
359	  title and toolbar background. If the toolbar should disappear entirely, use
360	  `.toolbarVisibility(.hidden, for: .windowToolbar)` instead.
361	- Remove custom toolbar backgrounds and manually painted titlebar fills before
362	  layering new SwiftUI toolbar APIs on top.
363	- Keep the window's logical title meaningful even if hidden; the system can
364	  still use it for accessibility and menu items. These are visual changes only.
365	
366	## Drag Regions
367	
368	- If a toolbar background is hidden or the toolbar is removed entirely, use
369	  `WindowDragGesture()` to extend the draggable area into your content.
370	- Attach the gesture to a transparent overlay or non-interactive header region
371	  that does not steal gestures from real controls.
372	- For a media player with custom playback controls, insert the drag overlay
373	  between the video content and the controls so AVKit or transport controls keep
374	  receiving input.
375	- Pair the drag gesture with `.allowsWindowActivationEvents(true)` so clicking
376	  and immediately dragging a background window still activates and moves it.
377	
378	## Background And Materials
379	
380	- Use `.containerBackground(.thickMaterial, for: .window)` when a utility window
381	  or About window should replace the default window background with a subtle
382	  frosted material.
383	- Prefer system materials for stylized windows instead of hardcoded translucent
384	  colors.
385	- Use this especially for fixed-content utility windows where a softer backdrop
386	  is part of the design.
387	
388	## Window Behavior
389	
390	- Use `.windowMinimizeBehavior(.disabled)` for always-reachable utility windows
391	  such as a custom About window where minimizing adds little value.
392	- Disable the green zoom control through fixed sizing or window constraints when
393	  the window's content has one intended size.
394	- Use `.restorationBehavior(.disabled)` for windows that should not reopen on
395	  next launch, such as About panels, transient support/info windows, or
396	  first-run welcome surfaces.
397	- Keep state restoration enabled for primary document or navigation windows when
398	  reopening prior size and position is desirable.
399	- By default, SwiftUI respects the user's system-wide macOS state restoration
400	  setting. Use `restorationBehavior(...)` only when a specific window should
401	  intentionally opt into or out of that system behavior.
402	- Use `.defaultLaunchBehavior(.presented)` for windows that should appear first
403	  on launch, such as a welcome window, and choose that behavior intentionally
404	  rather than relying on side effects from scene creation order.
405	
406	## Window Placement
407	
408	- Use `.defaultWindowPlacement { content, context in ... }` to control the
409	  initial size and optional position of newly opened windows.
410	- Inside the placement closure, call `content.sizeThatFits(.unspecified)` to get
411	  the content's ideal size.
412	- Read `context.defaultDisplay.visibleRect` to get the display's usable region
413	  after accounting for the menu bar and Dock.
414	- Return `WindowPlacement(size: size)` with a size clamped to the visible rect
415	  when media or document content may be larger than the display. If no position
416	  is provided, the window is centered by default.
417	- Use `.windowIdealPlacement { content, context in ... }` to control what
418	  happens when the user chooses Zoom from the Window menu or Option-clicks the
419	  green toolbar button. For media windows, preserve aspect ratio and grow to the
420	  largest size that fits the display.
421	- Treat default placement and ideal placement as separate policies:
422	  - default placement controls where a new window first appears,
423	  - ideal placement controls how large a zoomed window should become.
424	- Always consider external displays and rotated/narrow screens when sizing
425	  player windows or document windows from content dimensions.
426	
427	## Borderless And Specialized Windows
428	
429	- Use `.windowStyle(.plain)` for borderless or highly custom chrome windows, but
430	  make sure the content still provides a clear drag/move affordance and visible
431	  context.
432	- For a borderless player, HUD, or welcome window, decide upfront whether losing
433	  standard titlebar affordances is worth the custom presentation.
434	- Keep one clear path back to regular window management if the plain style makes
435	  the window feel invisible or hard to move.
436	
437	For concrete window modifier examples, read `references/api-snippets.md`.
438	
439	## Review Checklist
440	
441	- The scene type matches the window's role and lifecycle.
442	- Hidden titles still leave a meaningful logical title for accessibility and
443	  menus.
444	- Toolbar background removal is intentional and does not hurt titlebar legibility
445	  or window control placement.
446	- Windows with hidden or removed toolbars still have a reliable drag region and
447	  support click-then-drag activation from the background.
448	- Utility windows have restoration/minimize behavior that matches their purpose.
449	- Restoration overrides are used only when a scene should intentionally differ
450	  from the user's system-wide setting.
451	- Default and ideal placement use `content.sizeThatFits(.unspecified)` and
452	  `context.defaultDisplay.visibleRect` when content/display size matters.
453	- Media windows preserve aspect ratio and fit on small or rotated displays.
454	- Borderless windows still have a usable move/drag affordance.
455	
456	## Guardrails
457	
458	- Do not use `.toolbar(removing: .title)` just to hide a title you forgot to set.
459	  Keep the underlying window title meaningful.
460	- Do not hide the toolbar background or the whole toolbar without replacing the
461	  lost drag affordance.
462	- Do not disable restoration on the main document/navigation window unless the
463	  user explicitly wants a fresh-start app every launch.
464	- Do not hardcode one monitor size or assume a single-display setup when sizing
465	  player windows.
466	- Do not reach for `NSWindow` mutation before checking whether
467	  `.windowMinimizeBehavior`, `.restorationBehavior`, `.defaultWindowPlacement`,
468	  `.windowIdealPlacement`, `.windowStyle`, or `.defaultLaunchBehavior` already
469	  solve the problem.
470	- Do not leave a plain borderless window without any obvious drag or close path.
471	
472	## When To Use Other Skills
473	
474	- Use `swiftui-patterns` for broader scene, commands, settings, sidebar,
475	  and inspector architecture.
476	- Use `liquid-glass` when the main question is modern macOS visual treatment,
477	  Liquid Glass, or system material adoption.
478	- Use `appkit-interop` if a custom window behavior truly requires `NSWindow`,
479	  `NSPanel`, or responder-chain control.
480	- Use `build-run-debug` to launch and verify the resulting windows.
481	
482	########## view-refactor/SKILL.md ##########
483	---
484	name: view-refactor
485	description: Refactor macOS SwiftUI views and scenes into stable structure. Use when splitting large views, tightening scene state, or narrowing AppKit escapes.
486	---
487	
488	# View Refactor
489	
490	## Overview
491	
492	Refactor macOS views toward small, explicit, stable scene and view types. Default
493	to native SwiftUI for layout, selection, commands, and settings. Reach for AppKit
494	only at the narrow edges where desktop behavior truly requires it.
495	
496	## Core Guidelines
497	
498	### 1) Model scenes explicitly
499	
500	- Break the app into meaningful scene roots: main window, settings, utility windows, inspectors, or menu bar extras.
501	- Do not let one giant root view silently own every desktop surface.
502	
503	### 2) Keep a predictable file shape
504	
505	- Follow this ordering unless the file already has a stronger local convention:
506	- Environment
507	- `private`/`public` `let`
508	- `@State` / other stored properties
509	- computed `var` (non-view)
510	- `init`
511	- `body`
512	- computed view builders / other view helpers
513	- helper / async functions
514	
515	### 2b) Split files by responsibility
516	
517	- For non-trivial apps, do not keep the full app, all views, models, stores, networking clients, process clients, and helpers in one Swift file.
518	- Accept a single Swift file only for tiny throwaway examples or snippets: roughly under 50 lines, one screen, no persistence, no networking/process client, and no reusable models.
519	- Use `App/<AppName>App.swift` for the `@main` app and `AppDelegate` only.
520	- Keep `Views/ContentView.swift` focused on root layout and composition; move feature UI into files such as `Views/SidebarView.swift`, `Views/DetailView.swift`, and `Views/ComposerView.swift`.
521	- Move value types and selection enums into `Models/*.swift`, stores into `Stores/*.swift`, app-server/network/process clients into `Services/*.swift`, and small formatters/resolvers/extensions into `Support/*.swift`.
522	- Keep files small and named after the primary type they contain.
523	
524	### 3) Prefer dedicated subview types over many computed `some View` fragments
525	
526	- Extract meaningful desktop sections like sidebar rows, detail panels, inspectors, or toolbar content into focused subviews.
527	- Keep computed `some View` helpers small and rare.
528	- Pass explicit data, bindings, and actions into subviews instead of handing down the whole scene model.
529	
530	### 4) Keep selection and layout stable
531	
532	- Prefer one stable split or window layout with local conditionals inside it.
533	- Avoid top-level branch swapping between radically different roots when selection changes.
534	- Let the layout be constant; let state drive the content inside it.
535	
536	### 5) Extract commands, toolbars, and actions out of `body`
537	
538	- Do not bury non-trivial button logic inline.
539	- Do not mix command routing, menu state, and layout in the same block if they can be named clearly.
540	- Keep `body` readable as UI, not as a desktop view controller.
541	
542	### 6) Use scene and app storage intentionally
543	
544	- Use `@SceneStorage` for per-window ephemeral state when it truly helps restore the scene.
545	- Use `@AppStorage` for durable preferences, not transient UI toggles that only matter in one window.
546	- Keep scene-owned state close to the scene root.
547	
548	### 7) Keep AppKit escape hatches narrow
549	
550	- If a representable or `NSWindow` bridge exists, isolate it behind a small wrapper or helper.
551	- Do not let AppKit references spread through unrelated SwiftUI views.
552	- If the bridge starts owning the feature, re-evaluate the architecture.
553	
554	### 8) Observation usage
555	
556	- For `@Observable` reference types on modern macOS targets, store them as `@State` in the owning view.
557	- Pass observables explicitly to children.
558	- On older deployment targets, fall back to `@StateObject` and `@ObservedObject` where needed.
559	
560	## Workflow
561	
562	1. Identify the current scene boundary and whether the file is trying to do too much.
563	2. Reorder the file into a predictable top-to-bottom structure.
564	3. Extract desktop-specific sections into dedicated subview types.
565	4. Stabilize the root layout around selection, scenes, and commands rather than top-level branching.
566	5. Move action logic, command routing, and toolbar behavior into named helpers or separate types.
567	6. Tighten any AppKit bridge so the imperative edge is small and explicit.
568	7. Keep behavior intact unless the request explicitly asks for structural and behavioral changes together.
569	
570	## Refactor Checklist
571	
572	- Split oversized view files before adding more UI.
573	- Move pure models, identifiers, and selection enums out of view files.
574	- Move `Process`, `URLSession`, app-server, and platform client code out of SwiftUI views into `Services/`.
575	- Keep `AppDelegate` and the `@main` app entrypoint minimal.
576	- Build after each major split so compile errors stay local.
577	
578	## Common Smells
579	
580	- A root view that mixes window scaffolding, settings, toolbar code, command handling, and detail layout.
581	- A single app file that mixes app entrypoint, root layout, feature views, models, stores, service clients, and support extensions.
582	- iOS-style push navigation forced into a Mac sidebar-detail problem.
583	- Several booleans for mutually exclusive inspectors, sheets, or utility windows.
584	- AppKit objects passed through many SwiftUI layers without a clear ownership reason.
585	- Large computed view fragments standing in for real subviews.
586	
587	## Notes
588	
589	- A good macOS refactor should make scene structure, selection flow, and command ownership obvious.
590	- When the problem is fundamentally a missing desktop pattern, use `swiftui-patterns`.
591	- When the problem is fundamentally a boundary with AppKit, use `appkit-interop`.
592	
593	########## appkit-interop/SKILL.md ##########
594	---
595	name: appkit-interop
596	description: Bridge macOS SwiftUI into AppKit narrowly. Use when implementing representables, reaching NSWindow or panels, handling menus, or using the responder chain.
597	---
598	
599	# AppKit Interop
600	
601	## Quick Start
602	
603	Use this skill when SwiftUI is close but not quite enough for native macOS behavior.
604	Keep the bridge as small and explicit as possible. SwiftUI should usually remain
605	the source of truth, while AppKit handles the imperative edge.
606	
607	## Choose The Smallest Bridge
608	
609	- Use pure SwiftUI when the required behavior already exists in scenes, toolbars, commands, inspectors, or standard controls.
610	- Use `NSViewRepresentable` when you need a specific AppKit view with lightweight lifecycle needs.
611	- Use `NSViewControllerRepresentable` when you need controller lifecycle, delegation, or presentation coordination.
612	- Use direct AppKit window or app hooks when you need `NSWindow`, responder-chain, menu validation, panels, or app-level behavior.
613	
614	## Workflow
615	
616	1. Name the capability gap precisely.
617	   - Window behavior
618	   - Text system behavior
619	   - Menu validation
620	   - Drag and drop
621	   - File open/save panels
622	   - First responder control
623	
624	2. Pick the smallest boundary that solves it.
625	   - Avoid porting a whole screen to AppKit when one wrapped control or coordinator would do.
626	
627	3. Keep ownership explicit.
628	   - SwiftUI owns value state, selection, and observable models.
629	   - AppKit objects stay inside the representable, coordinator, or bridge object.
630	
631	4. Expose a narrow interface back to SwiftUI.
632	   - Bindings for editable state
633	   - Small callbacks for events
634	   - Focused bridge services only when necessary
635	
636	5. Validate lifecycle assumptions.
637	   - SwiftUI may recreate representables.
638	   - Coordinators exist to hold delegate and target-action glue, not as a second app architecture.
639	
640	## References
641	
642	- `references/representables.md`: choosing between view and view-controller wrappers, plus coordinator patterns.
643	- `references/window-panels.md`: window access, utility windows, and open/save panels.
644	- `references/responder-menus.md`: first responder, command routing, and menu validation.
645	- `references/drag-drop-pasteboard.md`: pasteboard, file URLs, and desktop drag/drop edges.
646	
647	## Guardrails
648	
649	- Do not duplicate the source of truth between SwiftUI and AppKit.
650	- Do not let `Coordinator` become an unstructured dumping ground.
651	- Do not store long-lived `NSView` or `NSWindow` instances globally without a strong ownership reason.
652	- Prefer a tiny tested bridge over rewriting the feature in raw AppKit.
653	- If a pattern can remain entirely in `swiftui-patterns`, keep it there.
654	
655	## Output Expectations
656	
657	Provide:
658	- the exact SwiftUI limitation being crossed
659	- the smallest recommended bridge type
660	- the data-flow boundary between SwiftUI and AppKit
661	- the lifecycle or validation risks to watch
662	
663

# UIUE 用户故事演绎台本 grill 决策（2026-06-25）

> grill-with-docs engineering-contract mode。从【用户故事演绎视角】拍 demo 台本：方案经理给客户现场演绎，怎么点/喊话/各屏幕元素表现。
> 承接 AD-11/AD-12 三屏分层 + D8/D13/D6。**避 PR7(codex rebuild-c6)冲突**：UIUE 专属文件，不碰 Makefile/docs/lessons-learned.md/docs/CURRENT.md/docs/research/INDEX.md/Core/Bench/Core/Contracts/contracts/。
> 术语 SSOT = `docs/uiue-ubiquitous-language.md`（避 PR7 改的根 UBIQUITOUS_LANGUAGE.md）。

---

## SD1 — 演绎开场起点 = idle 全景态（非每次 boot reveal）（2026-06-25 磊哥拍「同意推荐」）

**决策**：演绎开场客户第一眼 = **idle 全景态**（app 已开），非每次重启看 boot reveal。开场惊艳靠 idle 态本身质感（iOS26 + orb 呼吸 + 卡片质感），非开机动效。

**physical landing**：
- `AppLaunchMode`：`idle`（演绎默认态）/ `bootReveal`（仅真冷启动首次 ~2.5s phaseAnimator，非演绎主路径）。
- `Theme` **默认 `.ivory`（米白，🔴 磊哥 2026-06-25 改，原默认深灰）**；`.deepSpace`（深灰深空辉光）在设置调；**light/dark 双值跟随系统切换**（详 SD11）。
- `DemoResetControl`：一键归位干净 idle（演绎复位，防上个 demo 残留态；位置待设置面板 grill 定）。

**pre-mortem**：
- 🐯 tiger：每次重启演 boot reveal 拖节奏 → 台本用「app 已开直接喊」。
- 🐘 elephant（最值）：开场惊艳靠 idle 态质感（磊哥「too low」正是这个），非 boot reveal。
- frame-break：演绎连续性 → 需「演绎复位」入口（一键归位干净 idle）。

**承接**：D6 wow（boot reveal 降为冷启动首次）/ AD-12 §二三 zone（orb/content/mic）。

---

## SD2 — 喊话 = push-to-talk 按住录音 + iOS ASR 端侧转文字（后端只接文本）（2026-06-25 磊哥拍）

**决策**：话筒交互 = **push-to-talk 按住录音**（非我推荐的 tap-to-talk），松开 → **iOS SFSpeechRecognizer 端侧解析音频→文字** → 文字送后端（**后端只接 ASR 文本，不解析音频**）→ 中间对话区显示。
